metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the quickstart-command drift gate is (a) only preventing command execution breakage (probe-wired CI) or (b) also preventing README text drift from the correct syntax; if (b), add a mechanism that parses the README quickstart block and validates subcommand syntax or version strings against the canonical source (pyproject.toml / `rebar --help`).
findings.finding: The plan's chosen CI drift-proofing mechanism for 'quickstart-command rot' (wiring probe-rebar.sh into CI) validates command EXECUTION correctness but does not prevent the README quickstart TEXT from drifting — the two surfaces are independent. The probe uses dynamically-generated ticket IDs and internally-correct subcommand syntax; it cannot detect that the README still shows `rebar session-log "<entry>"` (missing `append` verb) or a stale alias. The named goal ('make quickstart-command rot fail the build automatically') requires a mechanism that checks README text accuracy, not just command executability.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Reuse note — 'New CI gates make quickstart-command rot … fail the build automatically' + 'scripts/probe-rebar.sh already implements the full install→…→close golden path; it just is not wired into CI.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a small CI step (or pytest parametrize) that extracts the bash code blocks from README.md's Quickstart section and validates: (1) each `rebar <subcommand>` matches a known subcommand from `rebar --help`, (2) `mcp>=X.Y` in the prose matches the floor in pyproject.toml. This complements probe-wired CI (which catches runtime breakage) and closes the text-drift gap.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one acceptance criterion that validates the human onboarding path end-to-end (e.g., a smoke-test script run in CI that executes the corrected README quickstart steps in a clean environment, or an explicit dogfooding note requiring a team member to follow the doc on a fresh clone before closing the epic).
findings.finding: No explicit human-onboarding validation mechanism is specified — the plan has a CI gate for drift but no step that confirms a new user can actually follow the corrected README end-to-end and arrive at a working session.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'move toward generated references' success criterion with a specific list of surfaces (e.g. dependency count in README, mcp floor in docs/config.md) and a binary done-signal (e.g. 'each named surface is emitted by a script and a CI drift gate rejects manual edits').
findings.finding: Success criterion 'Volatile command/tool surfaces move toward generated references so they cannot go stale by hand' is effort-framed ('move toward') rather than naming a specific observable end-state: which surfaces, and what done looks like.
findings.suggested_fix: State the default scope explicitly: 'The dependency count line in README and the mcp version floor in docs/config.md are emitted by a generator script; a CI drift gate (modelled on gen_env_registry.py) rejects manual edits to those lines.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the minimum observable form of the 'intent-based router' (e.g. 'a table or labelled section in docs/README.md with one row/entry per audience pointing to their primary entry point') so the criterion is evaluable without author interpretation.
findings.finding: Success criterion 'intent-based router for the four audiences' is subjective — no observable definition of what an acceptable router looks like is given, making in-session evaluation ambiguous.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the criterion: 'concretely, a table in docs/README.md with one row per audience (human client, LLM client, human contributor, LLM contributor) and a link to that audience's primary entry-point document.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a contributor-facing doc section (or at minimum a prominent comment block in each new gate script) explaining what each new CI drift gate checks, why it exists, and the exact command a contributor must run to regenerate/update the guarded reference when a legitimate change is made.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new CI drift-gate scripts (wiring probe-rebar.sh into CI, adding a docs-index drift gate modeled on gen_env_registry.py) as a new contributor-facing contract, but nowhere specifies that contributor documentation or an ADR will be produced explaining how these gates work, what they check, and how a contributor updates the generated reference when the guarded surface changes.
findings.location: Reuse note section; Success Criteria bullet 2 ('New CI gates make quickstart-command rot and docs-index drift fail the build automatically')
findings.suggested_fix: In the child ticket(s) that wire probe-rebar.sh and the docs-index gate into CI, add an explicit AC: 'CONTRIBUTING.md (or docs/development.md) documents the new drift gate: what it guards, the regeneration command, and when it is expected to fail.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a task to record the 'generator + drift-gate' docs CI pattern in CLAUDE.md or a lightweight ADR so future contributors understand the architecture of the new pipeline stage.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new CI drift-gate pipeline stage and a 'generator + drift-gate' pattern for docs content, but does not include a task to capture this architectural decision in CLAUDE.md, an ADR, or a design-doc update.
findings.location: Context > Reuse note; Success Criteria bullet 2 ('New CI gates make quickstart-command rot and docs-index drift fail the build automatically')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a child ticket or AC line: 'Update CLAUDE.md (or create docs/adr/drift-gates.md) documenting the generator+drift-gate pattern, which scripts implement each gate, and how to extend it for new generated references.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Sharpen the 'volatile surfaces → generated references' success criterion to name the specific surface(s) in scope (e.g., 'the quickstart command block in README is generated from probe-rebar.sh output') so done-ness is unambiguously observable.
findings.finding: The Success Criterion 'Volatile command/tool surfaces move toward generated references' is phrased aspirationally with no specific observable end-state, making it unclear when this goal is provably complete versus partially done.
findings.location: Success Criteria — fourth bullet ('Volatile command/tool surfaces move toward generated references')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Volatile command/tool surfaces move toward generated references so they cannot go stale by hand.' with a scoped, terminal statement such as: 'The README quickstart command block is generated/validated by a CI script (modelled on gen_env_registry.py); at least one additional volatile surface identified in child tickets is similarly gated.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the ordering dependency of zippy-trapezial-buzzard on the generator tickets (decorative-kingsize-degu, lupine-clearcut-mare) explicit — either as a formal 'blocks/blocked-by' relationship in the tracker or as a gating AC line in the trim ticket so it cannot merge before generated refs exist.
findings.finding: The dependency of zippy-trapezial-buzzard ('Trim duplicated command/config catalogs from README') on the generator tickets (decorative-kingsize-degu, lupine-clearcut-mare) completing first is implicit — it appears only in the child ticket's name ('after generated refs') but is not encoded as a formal sequencing constraint in the plan. If the trim ticket executes before the generators are merged, the README could temporarily lose reference content with no generated replacement yet in place.
findings.location: Child ticket: zippy-trapezial-buzzard; Success Criteria section; Reuse note
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC line to zippy-trapezial-buzzard: 'AC: decorative-kingsize-degu and lupine-clearcut-mare are merged and generated refs are live before this ticket is merged.' Alternatively, record a tracker dependency (this ticket is blocked by those two).

findings.finding: The plan silently drops the 'symptom->fix troubleshooting table' half of ticket 6755. The session log names the child as 'Add intent-based router + symptom->fix troubleshooting table to docs index,' but the plan only captures the intent-based router portion, with no mention of a troubleshooting table anywhere in Context, Success Criteria, or Acceptance Criteria.

The plan silently drops ticket b5a4 ('Trim duplicated command/config catalogs from README after generated refs are in place'). This cleanup step — removing hand-maintained duplicate content from README once generated references exist — appears as a discrete child ticket in the session log but is not mentioned anywhere in the plan's Context, Success Criteria, or Acceptance Criteria.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
